title: Silent catch{} as the root cause of claimed-to-ship bugs
body: writeSubstrateCache in server.ts had a bare catch{} that silently swallowed all runtime errors. The function was correctly defined, correctly called, and correctly compiled — but any exception (permission, JSON error, I/O) was invisible. This is the canonical anti-pattern: non-fatal error paths that produce no signal. Fix: ALWAYS log the error in catch, even for non-fatal operations. console.error('writeSubstrateCache FAILED: ' + String(err)) is the minimum bar.
author: Knight/Cursor

title: End-to-end test requirement for MCP side-effect writes
body: K520.6 tested writeSubstrateCache by inspection (reading the source) but never ran it end-to-end through the MCP protocol. The correct test for a server-side-effect: (1) delete the target file, (2) start the MCP server as a subprocess via stdio, (3) send the JSON-RPC request, (4) verify the file exists and has correct content. Unit tests of the function in isolation miss failures caused by the request pipeline, async/await issues, or server-startup state.
author: Knight/Cursor

title: Round-trip self-test as a structural integrity pattern
body: Added statSync(target) after writeFileSync to confirm the file actually landed on disk. If size=0, throw — don't silently succeed. This pattern (write then verify) closes the gap between 'I called writeFileSync' and 'the file is actually there'. Applicable to any disk-write in a non-fatal path: caches, audit logs, lock files, backup copies.
author: Knight/Cursor

title: briefingText truncation as defensive JSON payload sizing
body: writeSubstrateCache now truncates briefingText to 50,000 chars before JSON.stringify. The actual briefing is budget-enforced to ~1,000 words (~8,000 chars) by budgetEnforce(), so the truncation is a belt-and-suspenders guard against pathological payloads (e.g. if brief_me is called with a very large custom task string, or if budgetEnforce is bypassed in some code path). Total cache payload in a real session: ~21K bytes including gotchas.
author: Knight/Cursor

title: MCP stdio test client pattern for server verification
body: test_brief_me_k520_8.mjs demonstrates the correct pattern: spawn MCP server via stdio, send initialize + tools/call JSON-RPC sequence, capture stderr for [K520.8] diagnostic logs, check file side-effects after response. This is a portable pattern for any future server-side-effect verification. Key timing: 2000ms after init before sending tool call to allow server startup. Total test runtime: ~10s.
author: Knight/Cursor
